title: Rule-Based POS Tagging
concept: Early taggers used hand-written grammar rules, which were highly precise but brittle and struggled with slang or new words.

exercises.prompt: What does a Part of Speech (POS) tagger look for in text Speech (POS) tagger label in a sentence?
exercises.explain: A POS (Part of Speech) tagger identifies the grammatical category of words, such as nouns, verbs, and adjectives.

cards.title: Hand-Coded Grammar
cards.body: Before AI could learn on its own, human linguists had to write down thousands of grammar rules by hand to teach computers how to read. Imagine writing a rule for every single scenario! Let's see how they did it and why it eventually broke.

cards.title: Rule-Based POS Tagging
cards.body: Rule-Based POS Tagging is an early method of finding parts of speech by using a list of hand-written grammar rules. For example, a rule might say: 'If a word follows "the", it is a noun.' While highly accurate for standard sentences, these systems are very rigid.

cards.title: The Strict Librarian
cards.body: Imagine hiring a strict librarian who only categorizes books using a giant, unchangeable handbook. If you hand them a traditional book, they organize it perfectly. But if you hand them a modern sci-fi novel with invented slang, they freeze and don't know what to do because it is not in their handbook.

cards.title: The Twitter Test
cards.body: A rule-based tagger works perfectly on standard text like: 'The quick brown fox jumps.' But if you feed it a tweet like: 'That movie was fire!', the tagger gets confused because its rules say 'fire' is only a noun, not an adjective meaning 'excellent'.

cards.title: Are Rules Outdated?
cards.body: Many think that rule-based systems are useless today because they are old. In reality, they are still used to clean up data or enforce strict grammatical rules where 100% accuracy is needed for standard language. They just need to be paired with smarter systems!

cards.title: Rigid Rules vs. Live Language
cards.body: See how rule-based taggers struggle with new, creative language compared to traditional structures.

left.text: Sentence: 'The economy grew.' -> Rules work perfectly because grammar is standard.

right.text: Sentence: 'That track is fire!' -> Rules fail because 'fire' is used as an adjective.

exercises.prompt: What is the main limitation of a rule-based POS tagger?

options.text: They automatically update their rules when they see new words.
They are too slow to run on modern computers.
They cannot handle slang or new words because their rules are fixed.
They only work with numbers, not words.

exercises.explain: Rule-based taggers rely on fixed dictionary lookups and written grammar rules, so they cannot adapt to words or usage styles they have never seen before.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Rule-based POS taggers automatically learn new rules on their own without human help.
exercises.explain: Rule-based systems require humans to manually write rules, while machine learning systems learn patterns automatically from data.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe the main weakness of rule-based taggers.
exercises.sentence: Rule-based taggers are highly precise for standard grammar, but they are very ________ when dealing with slang or new words.
exercises.explain: Rule-based taggers are brittle because they depend entirely on static rules and cannot adapt to language shifts or slang without human intervention.

exercises.prompt: Which of these sentences is a rule-based tagger most likely to tag incorrectly?
exercises.explain: The sentence 'This setup is fire!' uses 'fire' as slang for 'cool' or 'excellent'. A rigid rule-based tagger would likely misclassify it as a noun because it only knows the literal definition.

exercises.prompt: Why do modern NLP systems prefer machine learning taggers over old rule-based taggers when reading social media posts?

options.text: Rule-based systems automatically learn from their mistakes over time.
Rule-based systems are faster at understanding internet slang.
Rule-based systems struggle with new words because they only know fixed, hand-written rules.
Rule-based systems use neural networks to guess the meaning of new words.

exercises.explain: Rule-based POS taggers fail on words they haven't seen because they rely on fixed, manual rules. Machine learning taggers, however, learn patterns from data and can guess the part of speech for brand new words based on context!
